title: Services, EndpointSlices, DNS, Discovery, and Traffic Policies · Changed-Cluster Lab
summary: An independent delivery team receives an unfamiliar namespace, failure domain, and policy constraint and must transfer services, endpointslices, dns, discovery, and traffic policies into a new service discovery and endpoint evidence map. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of service discovery and endpoint evidence map with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence de…
objectives: Explain Pod IP identity, node routing, ports, host network exceptions, replacement, dual-stack boundaries, and application bind behavior.
Trace Service selectors, EndpointSlices, readiness and serving conditions, manual endpoints, topology hints, and stale endpoint diagnosis.
Choose ClusterIP, headless, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName, or selectorless Service from discovery, exposure, platform, and security constraints.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Pod Network Identity
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab Service selector matches the wrong Pods, EndpointSlices show no ready endpoints, a client calls localhost, and DNS search expansion reaches an unintended namespace. Trace selector, readiness, virtual IP, EndpointSlice, port mapping, DNS, and session behavior. Competency probe: Explain Pod IP identity, node routing, ports, host network exceptions, replacement, dual-stack boundaries, and application bind behavior.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Pod Network Identity.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for explain pod ip identity, node routing, ports, host network exceptions, replacement, dual-stack boundaries, and application bind behavior. For Commit to the mission model · Lab K8s Pod Network Identity, keep K8s Pod Network Identity connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy
steps.instruction:
Append the Kubernetes evidence item headed "  # Evidence: k8s-traffic-session-locality-policy-1jkvgl". Tailor its service-discovery.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic API or manifest review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run kubectl, Helm, kubeadm, and cluster commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy, keep K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-traffic-session-locality-policy-1jkvgl
  - competency: k8s-traffic-session-locality-policy
    artifact: service-discovery.yaml
    decision: traffic session locality policy
    environment: shared multi-tenant cluster under Restricted Pod Security
    fault-injection: delete one ready Pod during a configuration rollout
    retained-proof: Pod state previous exit endpoint readiness and request result
    case-key: 1jkvgl
    change: |
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: Service
      metadata:
      name: status-api
      spec:
      type: ClusterIP
      selector: {app: status-api}
      endpoint-evidence: ready-serving-terminating
      dns: status-api.intake.svc.cluster.local
    verify: kubectl get service,endpointslice -l kubernetes.io/service-name=status-api -o json
    failure: Reject Pod IP pinning, short-name cross-namespace assumptions, Service-as-health-probe claims, or locality policy as automatic availability.
    repair: Align selectors, inspect EndpointSlice conditions, use qualified DNS, test bind and route layers, and document platform-specific exposure.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, copy one familiar manifest or kubectl command, omit context, API discovery, desired-versus-observed status, failure, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer checks, then accept an Applied response as production proof.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy
steps.instruction:
Write the lab evidence note for K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A lab Service selector matches the wrong Pods, EndpointSlices show no ready endpoints, a client calls localhost, and DNS search expansion reaches an unintended namespace. Trace selector, readiness, virtual IP, EndpointSlice, port mapping, DNS, and session behavior. Competency probe: Evaluate session affinity, internal and external traffic policies, source preservation, locality, health checks, uneven load, and failure-domain tradeoffs.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy, keep K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Evaluate session affinity, internal and external traffic policies, source preservation, locality, health checks, uneven load, and failure-domain tradeoffs.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy, name the context, namespace, API identity, desired state, observed status, controller, policy, and failure-domain boundary, K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy, trace this concrete input and predict admission, defaulting, scheduling, reconciliation, status, traffic, storage, policy, disruption, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A lab Service selector matches the wrong Pods, EndpointSlices show no ready endpoints, a client calls localhost, and DNS search expansion reaches an unintended namespace. Trace selector, readiness, virtual IP, EndpointSlice, port mapping, DNS, and session behavior. Competency probe: Evaluate session affinity, internal and external traffic policies, source preservation, locality, health checks, uneven load, and failure-domain tradeoffs.

For Defend the transferred model · Lab K8s Traffic Session Locality Policy, challenge the documented misconception with object, condition, event, policy, failure, repair, rollback, and transfer evidence: Local traffic policy always improves availability and balances requests evenly.
